title: Adaptable Continuous Variable Quantum Network with Finite Size Security
authors: Runjia Zhang, Akash nag Oruganti, Huy Q Nguyen, Adnan A. E. Hajomer, Vladyslav C. Usenko, Ulrik L. Andersen, Tobias Gehring
abstract: In recent years, continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) has become a promising paradigm for enabling secure communication among multiple end users sharing the same telecommunication backbone. CV-QKD with reverse reconciliation naturally enables scalability from conventional point-to-point links to quantum access networks based on passive quantum broadcasting channels. Here, we report an experimental demonstration on an active $1:4$ multi-user CV quantum network (QN) in the finite-size regime. With $1.25\cdot10^9$ coherent states exchanged on each $11\text{km}$ quantum channel, the highest performance for secret key generation totaling $1.9\cdot10^{-1}$ bits/channel use. Furthermore, we investigate adaptable CV-QN protocols that comprehensively allow network operation in various security and key rates requirements of individual users. The results establish the practical security of CV-QN compatible with existing telecommunication for broad deployment, and allowing additional degree of freedom for connected end users in existing infrastructures.
summary: The researchers demonstrated a 1-to-4 continuous-variable quantum network that can adapt its security level based on user trust. By accounting for finite-size effects, they achieved high-performance secret key generation compatible with existing telecommunications infrastructure.
main_problem: Scaling continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) from point-to-point links to multi-user access networks while addressing finite-size security and protocol adaptability.
main_result: Experimental demonstration of an active 1:4 CV quantum network achieving a total secret key rate of 1.9e-1 bits/channel use, with a framework that allows for different security/trust levels.
method: A unified Gaussian security framework using a chain-rule decomposition to assign joint key contributions to individual users, combined with DSP-based phase recovery and error correction.
model_or_system: An active 1:4 multi-user continuous-variable quantum network using a passive optical network (PON) topology with a 1:4 beam splitter, 11 km fiber channels, and coherent state modulation.
key_observables: Secret key rate (per-user and total), mutual information, Holevo information, and channel excess noise.
important_parameters: 1.25e9 coherent states exchanged, 125 MBaud symbol rate, modulation variance V_M = 5 SNU, reconciliation efficiency beta = 0.95, and 11 km channel length.
main_assumptions: Gaussian attacks (collective attacks), finite-size data block limitations, and varying trust assumptions (trusted, collaborative, or untrusted users).
figures_summary: Figure 1a illustrates the 1:4 network topology and the different protocol modes; Figure 3 shows finite-size secret key rates versus the number of signals for the three trust scenarios.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the multi-user CV-QKD problem, describes the experimental setup and hardware, presents the security framework and key rate decomposition, details the experimental results across different trust modes, and concludes with a performance comparison against existing networks.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information due to its implementation of multi-user entanglement-based communication and the development of scalable, hardware-compatible quantum network protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) Trust assumptions of different broadcasting CV-QKD protocols in access CV-QKD network. Dashed areas indicate ele- ments that are not trusted under respective protocol. Under the trusted protocol quantum channels are not trusted, while users have a varying degree of trust between them. The collaborative protocol relies on measured results of other users to be kept private from Eve, while their detectors are not necessarily trusted. All non-reference Bobs are assumed to be fully a part of Eve in the untrusted protocol. (b) Per-user secret key rate as a function of channel loss for a multi-user CV-QKD network with uniform splitting among users. The solid curves correspond to the...

Fig. 2. A continuous variable quantum access network in action in the lab. (a) State preparation station comprises a signal laser, a digital-to-analog converter (DAC), an IQ modulator stabilized by an automatic bias controller (ABC), as well as a variable optical attenuator (VOA) and an isolator. (b) State distribution is assisted with a 1 : 4 splitter which splits the coherent states, forming a quantum broadcasting channel that connects N users while distributing quantum correlations evenly among them. The link consists of two segment, a 10 km fiber spool before the 1 : 4 splitter represent the communication backbone and at each output of the splitter represent last-mile connection to the...

Fig. 3. Finite-size secret key rates as a function of the total number of exchanged quantum signals for the different trust scenarios considered in the multi-user CV-QKD network. The solid curves correspond to the finite-size secret key rates, while the dashed horizontal lines indicate the corresponding asymptotic secret key rates. (Left) Trusted-user scenario, where non-reference users are assumed trusted and excluded from Eve’s system. (Middle) Collaborative (conditionally trusted) scenario, where auxiliary users publicly disclose their measurements to improve the effective correlations used for key extraction.
